skill.name: dittobot
skill.description: Rewrite, edit, tighten, punch up, or diagnose user-provided prose while preserving the user's voice, intent, facts, stance, rhythm, humor, and formality. Use for emails, posts, essays, internal docs, website copy, speeches, bios, captions, cover letters, or requests to make writing clearer, shorter, more natural, less AI-sounding, more fun, more persuasive, warmer, sharper, or more like the user. Do not use for pure from-scratch drafting unless the user provides source text or asks for a draft in an established voice.
skill.summary: Voice-preserving prose rewriting and editing skill that tightens, punches up, and polishes user writing while keeping their authentic tone, facts, and style intact.
skill.author: RegionallyFamous

security_audit.summary: Dittobot is a voice-preserving prose rewriting skill with standard installation and evaluation tooling. The static analyzer flagged 490 patterns, but the vast majority are false positives from markdown backtick formatting in documentation files (README, CHANGELOG, CONTRIBUTING, SKILL.md) being misread as shell execution. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings refer to hash operations in Python evaluation/scoring scripts, not security-sensitive cryptography. The install script uses a curl|bash pattern which is conventional for skill installation but worth noting. No confirmed malicious intent; risk is medium due to install scripts, API access in live_eval.py, and overall tooling surface area.

high_findings.title: Install script uses curl pipe to shell pattern
high_findings.description: README.md:187 documents and install.sh uses a curl|bash installation pattern, which executes remote code directly. While standard for skill distribution, this pattern carries inherent supply chain risk if the remote URL is compromised.

medium_findings.title: Live evaluation script accesses environment variables for API keys
medium_findings.description: scripts/live_eval.py reads multiple environment variables that appear to be API keys (for LLM provider authentication). This is expected for a skill that evaluates writing quality via API calls, but it means the skill requires and handles credentials.

medium_findings.title: Installation scripts perform shell substitution and file operations
medium_findings.description: install.sh and scripts/install.py use command substitution ($()) and file system operations (shutil, symlink creation, hidden file access in home directory). These are standard for installation tooling but represent execution surface area.

medium_findings.title: Redaction script references crypto seed patterns for detection
medium_findings.description: scripts/redact_case.py references crypto seed/private key patterns as part of its redaction logic. This is the tool detecting and redacting sensitive patterns, not storing actual keys, but it shows the script handles sensitive pattern matching.

low_findings.title: Hash operations in scoring and evaluation scripts
low_findings.description: Multiple Python scripts (scorecard.py, live_eval.py, audit.py, etc.) use hash functions. These are used for content comparison and scoring, not for security-sensitive operations. The static analyzer flags these as 'weak cryptographic algorithm' but they serve non-security purposes.

low_findings.title: Markdown backtick formatting detected as shell execution
low_findings.description: The overwhelming majority of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are false positives. Markdown files (README.md, CHANGELOG.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, SKILL.md, etc.) use backticks for inline code formatting, which the static analyzer misinterprets as shell command execution patterns. These are documentation artifacts, not executable code.

low_findings.title: Dynamic import expression in report generation script
low_findings.description: scripts/rewrite_report.py:14 uses a dynamic import() expression. This appears to be intentional for the skill's report generation module loading, not a security threat.

dangerous_patterns.title: Curl pipe to shell (install pattern)
dangerous_patterns.description: Remote code execution via curl|bash installation pattern documented in README

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess execution in build and install scripts
dangerous_patterns.description: Python subprocess.run and shell command substitution used in build/install tooling

content.user_title: Rewrite Any Prose While Keeping Your Voice
content.value_statement: AI rewriting tools often flatten your unique voice into generic, corporate-sounding text. Dittobot preserves your tone, facts, rhythm, and intent while making your writing clearer, sharper, or more natural.
actual_capabilities: Rewrite emails, posts, essays, and documents while preserving your authentic voice
Tighten and punch up verbose or flat prose into sharper, more engaging text
Make writing less AI-sounding and more natural and human
Adapt tone to be warmer, funnier, more persuasive, or more formal on request
Diagnose writing problems and explain what is working or not working
Maintain factual accuracy, stance, and intent across all rewrites
limitations: Not designed for pure from-scratch drafting without source material or voice reference
Cannot verify factual claims beyond what is present in the source text
Works best when the user provides clear direction on desired tone or style changes
May not capture highly idiosyncratic or experimental writing styles perfectly

use_cases.title: Polishing professional emails
use_cases.description: Rewrite work emails to be clearer, more confident, and appropriately formal while keeping your natural voice.

use_cases.title: Sharpening blog posts and essays
use_cases.description: Tighten drafts, cut filler, add punch, and ensure the writing sounds like a real person, not a chatbot.

use_cases.title: Editing cover letters and bios
use_cases.description: Make application materials stand out by matching the tone of the role while keeping your personality visible.

prompt_templates.title: Basic rewrite request
prompt_templates.prompt:
Rewrite this email to be clearer and more direct, but keep my voice:

[paste your text here]
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick tightening of a message you already wrote

prompt_templates.title: Tone adjustment
prompt_templates.prompt:
Take this draft and make it sound warmer and less corporate. Keep all the facts and the main point exactly the same:

[paste your text here]

prompt_templates.title: Full punch-up with direction
prompt_templates.prompt:
Rewrite this blog post section. Goals: shorter sentences, cut filler, add one vivid detail, keep my dry humor, do not change any facts:

[paste your text here]
prompt_templates.scenario: Detailed rewrite with specific style instructions

prompt_templates.title: Diagnostic mode
prompt_templates.prompt:
Do not rewrite this yet. Diagnose what is not working in this piece and list the top 3 issues with specific examples from the text:

[paste your text here]
prompt_templates.scenario: Getting feedback before deciding on edits

output_examples.input: Rewrite this to be less formal: 'I am writing to inquire about the status of my application. I submitted my materials on the 15th and would appreciate an update at your earliest convenience.'
output_examples.output: Quick follow-up on my application, submitted on the 15th. Any update on where things stand?

output_examples.input: Punch up this sentence: 'The product has many features that users find useful in their daily work.'
output_examples.output: The product does the boring stuff so you do not have to.

output_examples.input: Diagnose what is wrong with this paragraph: 'Our company is a leader in the industry. We have many solutions. Our team is dedicated to excellence. We serve many clients across many sectors.'
output_examples.output: Three main problems: (1) Every sentence starts the same way, creating a flat rhythm. (2) Vague words like 'many', 'solutions', 'excellence' carry no specific meaning. (3) There is no concrete proof, story, or detail. Fix: vary sentence openings, replace abstract nouns with one specific example, and cut at least two of the four sentences.

best_practices: Always provide source text or an established voice reference before asking for rewrites
Specify the desired change direction clearly (shorter, warmer, funnier, more formal)
State what must not change: facts, stance, specific phrases, or named entities
Use diagnostic mode first when you are unsure what needs to change
anti_patterns: Do not ask Dittobot to draft a full piece from a blank page with no source material
Do not use it to fabricate facts or add claims not present in your original text
Do not apply it to highly technical or legal text where precision is critical and ambiguity is dangerous

faq.question: What makes Dittobot different from a standard AI rewriter?
faq.answer: Dittobot is designed to preserve your voice rather than replace it. It keeps your rhythm, humor, stance, and facts intact while improving clarity and impact.

faq.question: Can I use it for from-scratch writing?
faq.answer: Dittobot works best when you provide source text to rewrite or an established voice to match. For pure drafting, provide a sample of your writing or a voice profile.

faq.question: Will it change my facts or meaning?
faq.answer: Dittobot is instructed to preserve facts, stance, and intent. You can reinforce this by explicitly stating what must not change in your prompt.

faq.question: What types of writing does it handle?
faq.answer: Emails, blog posts, essays, internal documents, website copy, speeches, bios, captions, cover letters, and social media posts.

faq.question: Can I ask for multiple style adjustments at once?
faq.answer: Yes. You can request combinations like 'shorter and funnier' or 'more formal but warmer' in a single prompt.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. Dittobot is compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as a reusable writing skill.
